title: Exponents & Roots
blurb: Master exponent rules, radicals, scientific notation, and how powers and roots behave between 0 and 1.
theory:
What Exponents Mean

An exponent counts repeated multiplication. In the expression bn, b is the base and n is the exponent (or power). So 24 = 2×2×2×2 = 16. Reading this carefully is the foundation for every rule below.

The Core Exponent Rules

All of these apply only when the bases match (except the last two).

RuleFormulaExample
Productbm × bn = bm+n23 × 24 = 27
Quotientbm ÷ bn = bm−n56 ÷ 52 = 54
Power of a power(bm)n = bm×n(32)4 = 38
Power of a product(ab)n = anbn(2x)3 = 8x3
Power of a quotient(a/b)n = an/bn(3/4)2 = 9/16

Critical: there is no rule for adding or subtracting different powers. 23 + 24 is NOT 27; it equals 8 + 16 = 24.

Zero and Negative Exponents

Any nonzero base to the zero power equals 1: b0 = 1. So 70 = 1 and (−9)0 = 1.

A negative exponent means take the reciprocal: b−n = 1/bn. For example 2−3 = 1/23 = 1/8. A negative exponent never makes the value negative; it makes it a fraction (for bases greater than 1).

Fractional Exponents

A fractional exponent is a root. The denominator is the root and the numerator is the power: bm/n = the n-th root of bm. So:

  • b1/2 = √b
  • b1/3 = cube root of b
  • 163/4 = (161/4)3 = 23 = 8

Take the root first when the base is a perfect power; it keeps numbers small.

Scientific Notation

Scientific notation writes a number as a × 10n, where 1 ≤ a < 10 and n is an integer. Move the decimal: a positive exponent shifts right (big numbers), a negative exponent shifts left (small numbers).

  • 45,000 = 4.5 × 104
  • 0.00067 = 6.7 × 10−4

To multiply, multiply the leading numbers and add the exponents: (3 × 104)(2 × 10−7) = 6 × 10−3.

Square Roots and Cube Roots

The square root √a is the nonnegative number whose square is a. Memorize perfect squares through 152 = 225 and the small cubes: 23=8, 33=27, 43=64, 53=125. On the GRE, √a means the positive root only, so √9 = 3 (not ±3). But if you solve x2 = 9, then x = 3 or x = −3.

Simplifying Radicals

Pull out perfect-square factors: √(a×b) = √a × √b. For example √50 = √(25×2) = √25 × √2 = 5√2. Always factor out the largest perfect square so the number left under the root is as small as possible.

Adding and Multiplying Radicals

You can only add radicals that have the same radicand (the number under the root), treating them like like terms: 2√3 + 5√3 = 7√3. But √2 + √3 cannot be combined. To add √8 + √2, first simplify: √8 = 2√2, so √8 + √2 = 2√2 + √2 = 3√2.

Multiplication is freer: √a × √b = √(a×b). So √6 × √2 = √12 = 2√3.

Rationalizing Denominators

A radical in the denominator is rewritten by multiplying top and bottom by that radical: 6/√3 = (6×√3)/(√3×√3) = 6√3/3 = 2√3. When the denominator is a sum like √5 − 2, multiply by the conjugate √5 + 2, which produces a difference of squares: 1/(√5 − 2) = (√5 + 2)/((√5)2 − 22) = (√5 + 2)/(5 − 4) = √5 + 2.

Comparing Powers and Roots

To compare powers with different bases and exponents, rewrite them with a common base or a common exponent. To compare 230 and 320, write both with exponent 10: 230 = (23)10 = 810 and 320 = (32)10 = 910. Since 9 > 8, 320 is larger.

Roots of Fractions and Decimals (0 < x < 1)

This is the GRE's favorite trap. When 0 < x < 1, the usual intuition reverses:

  • Raising to a power makes it smaller: (1/2)3 = 1/8 < 1/2. Higher powers give smaller values.
  • Taking a root makes it larger: √(1/4) = 1/2 > 1/4. A root pulls the value up toward 1.

So for 0 < x < 1: x3 < x2 < x < √x. For x > 1 the order flips back to normal. Always check whether the base is between 0 and 1 before comparing.
whatEtsTests:
• Applying the product, quotient, and power-of-power rules together in one expression, especially recognizing that different powers cannot be added directly.
  • Solving exponential equations by rewriting both sides with the same base (e.g., 2x = 8x−2).
  • Negative and fractional exponents converted between root and reciprocal form.
  • Simplifying radicals and combining like radicals (√8 + √2 = 3√2) and rationalizing denominators including conjugates.
  • Comparing large powers with different bases using common bases or common exponents in Quantitative Comparison questions.
  • The 0 < x < 1 reversal: powers shrink, roots grow. This appears constantly in QC traps.
  • Scientific notation arithmetic, particularly multiplying and dividing with negative exponents.
tips: To compare powers, force a common base or common exponent — never compute the full giant number.
For fractional exponents, take the root first (the denominator), then apply the power; numbers stay tiny.
A negative exponent just flips the base to its reciprocal: b^(−n) = 1/b^n — it does not create a negative number.
Memorize squares to 15^2=225 and cubes to 5^3=125 so you recognize them instantly under a radical.
Before combining radicals, simplify each one fully — hidden like terms (√8 = 2√2) often appear.
When the base is between 0 and 1, flip your instinct: higher powers are smaller, roots are larger.
traps: Adding different powers as if they had a rule: 2^3 + 2^4 ≠ 2^7. You must compute 8 + 16 = 24.
Thinking a negative exponent gives a negative number; 3^(−2) = 1/9, a positive fraction.
Combining unlike radicals: √2 + √3 cannot be simplified to √5 or any single root.
Forgetting the 0<x<1 reversal and assuming x^2 > x or √x < x when x is a proper fraction.
Reading √9 as ±3 on the GRE; the radical symbol denotes only the positive root (3), even though x^2=9 has two solutions.

examples.steps:
1. Use the product rule on the numerator: 23 × 25 = 23+5 = 28.

2. Use the quotient rule: 28 ÷ 22 = 28−2 = 26.

3. Evaluate: 26 = 64.
examples.shortcut: Just add and subtract the exponents in one pass: 3 + 5 − 2 = 6, so the answer is 26 = 64.

examples.steps:
1. The denominator 4 is the root, the numerator 3 is the power.

2. Take the 4th root first: 161/4 = 2 (since 24 = 16).

3. Raise to the 3rd power: 23 = 8.
examples.shortcut: Root before power keeps numbers small: 161/4 = 2, then 23 = 8. Doing the power first would force you through 163 = 4096.

examples.steps:
1. Factor out perfect squares: √8 = √(4×2) = 2√2.

2. √18 = √(9×2) = 3√2.

3. Now they share the radicand √2, so add the coefficients: 2√2 + 3√2 = 5√2.
examples.shortcut: Both reduce to multiples of √2 (2√2 and 3√2). Add coefficients: 5√2.

examples.steps:
1. Multiply numerator and denominator by √5: (10 × √5)/(√5 × √5).

2. The denominator becomes √5 × √5 = 5.

3. Result: 10√5/5 = 2√5.

questions.explain: Same base, so add exponents: 25 × 23 = 25+3 = 28. The base stays 2; do not multiply the bases to get 4.
√64 = 8 because 82 = 64. On the GRE the radical denotes the positive root only.

questions.q: What is the value of 2−3 expressed as a decimal? Enter your answer.
questions.explain: A negative exponent means reciprocal: 2−3 = 1/23 = 1/8 = 0.125. The result is positive, not negative.
Rewrite with a common exponent of 10. Quantity A: 230 = (23)10 = 810. Quantity B: 320 = (32)10 = 910. Since 9 > 8, Quantity B is greater. The answer is B.
The denominator is the root: 161/4 = 2. The numerator is the power: 23 = 8. So 163/4 = 8.

questions.q: Compute (4 × 105)(3 × 10−2) and express it in standard form.

questions.explain: Factor out the largest perfect square: 98 = 49 × 2. So √98 = √49 × √2 = 7√2.

questions.q: When 12/√6 is rationalized and fully simplified, it has the form k√6. What is the value of k? Enter your answer.
questions.explain: Multiply top and bottom by √6: (12 × √6)/(√6 × √6) = 12√6/6 = 2√6. So k = 2.

questions.q: It is given that 0 < x < 1. Compare the two quantities.
questions.explain: For a value between 0 and 1, a root pulls the number up toward 1 while squaring pushes it down toward 0. For example x = 1/4 gives √x = 1/2 and x2 = 1/16, so √x > x2. This holds for every x in (0,1). The answer is A.

questions.q: Which of the following expressions are equal to 1/8? Indicate all that apply.
If 2x+1 = 8x−1, what is the value of x?
questions.explain: Rewrite 8 as 23: 8x−1 = 23(x−1) = 23x−3. With equal bases, set exponents equal: x + 1 = 3x − 3, so 4 = 2x and x = 2. Check: 23 = 8 and 81 = 8.
